question: Which structure lets me read the thousandth element instantly but has to copy everything over when it grows past its capacity?
ideal_answer: A dynamic array: O(1) indexed access, but growing past capacity allocates a bigger block and copies the elements.

question: Which sequence type gives constant-time access by position and the best cache locality, but O(n) cost to insert in the middle?
ideal_answer: A dynamic array, because elements are contiguous and indexed directly, but middle insertion shifts later elements.

question: In which structure can I remove a known element just by rewiring a couple of pointers, even though reaching an arbitrary position is slow?
ideal_answer: A linked list: O(1) splice at a known node, but O(n) to reach a position because you must walk from the head.

question: In which structure does getting to the k-th element mean walking node by node from the start, following pointers?
ideal_answer: A linked list, which has no index arithmetic, so positional access is O(n) by traversing from the head.

question: Which ordered tree gives logarithmic operations only when keys happen to arrive in a balanced order, with no self-balancing of its own?
ideal_answer: A plain binary search tree, which keeps the left-smaller/right-larger order but does not rebalance, so skewed input degrades it to O(n).

question: Which ordered tree can collapse into a linked-list shape if you insert already-sorted keys into it?
ideal_answer: A plain (unbalanced) binary search tree, which becomes skewed and O(n) when keys arrive in sorted order.

question: Which tree restores a height-balance condition with rotations after every insertion to guarantee worst-case logarithmic operations?
ideal_answer: An AVL tree, a self-balancing BST that rotates to keep subtree heights within one, guaranteeing O(log n).

question: Which structure stores a balance factor at each node and rebalances itself on updates?
ideal_answer: An AVL tree, which tracks each node's balance factor and performs rotations to stay strictly height-balanced.

question: Which structure only orders each parent against its own children and is used to always serve the highest-priority item next?
ideal_answer: A binary heap, which satisfies the heap property between parent and children and implements a priority queue.

question: Which complete tree is stored compactly in an array and exposes the minimum at its root in constant time?
ideal_answer: A binary heap, a complete tree kept in an array where the root is the min (or max), read in O(1).

question: Which in-place divide-and-conquer sort can fall to quadratic time when the split element is chosen badly?
ideal_answer: Quicksort, which partitions around a pivot in place but degrades to O(n^2) on a poor pivot choice.

question: Which fast average-case sort needs almost no extra memory but does not preserve the order of equal elements?
ideal_answer: Quicksort: in-place with O(log n) stack space and O(n log n) average time, but not stable.

question: Which divide-and-conquer sort keeps equal elements in their original order and needs extra space proportional to the input?
ideal_answer: Merge sort, which is stable and uses an O(n) auxiliary array.

question: Which sort guarantees n-log-n time even in the worst case, at the cost of an auxiliary array?
ideal_answer: Merge sort, which guarantees O(n log n) in all cases but is not in place.

question: In which collision scheme does each bucket hold a list of all the entries that hashed to it?
ideal_answer: Separate chaining, where each bucket holds a linked list of colliding entries.

question: Which hashing approach can keep working with a load factor above one and tolerates frequent deletions gracefully?
ideal_answer: Separate chaining, whose buckets can hold many entries, so the load factor can exceed one and deletions are easy.

question: Which collision scheme stores every entry directly in the table and finds a free slot by probing a sequence?
ideal_answer: Open addressing, which probes (linear, quadratic, or double hashing) for an empty slot within the same array.

question: Which hashing approach needs tombstone markers on deletion and must keep its load factor well below one?
ideal_answer: Open addressing, which requires empty slots for probing to terminate and tombstones so probe sequences survive deletion.

question: Why is appending to the end usually cheap even though a single append can occasionally cost O(n)?
ideal_answer: A dynamic array grows by doubling and copying, which spreads the cost so appends are amortized O(1) despite the occasional O(n) resize.

question: Which container do languages typically surface as a vector, an ArrayList, or a list?
ideal_answer: A dynamic array, exposed under names like vector, ArrayList, or list.

question: For tight numerical loops doing lots of random reads by index, which structure offers the best cache behavior?
ideal_answer: A dynamic array: contiguous storage and direct index arithmetic give O(1) reads and excellent cache locality.

question: Which structure carries no per-element overhead, storing just the values in one compact block?
ideal_answer: A dynamic array, which stores only the values contiguously with no per-element pointer overhead.

question: Which variant carries a backward pointer so a known node can be deleted in O(1) without first scanning for its predecessor?
ideal_answer: A doubly linked list, whose backward pointers let a known node be removed without finding its predecessor first.

question: Which structure suffers poor cache locality because its elements are scattered across memory instead of being contiguous?
ideal_answer: A linked list, whose nodes are scattered (not contiguous), giving poor cache locality.

question: In which structure is a single reference to the first element the only entry point?
ideal_answer: A linked list, where the head reference is the only way into the structure.

question: Which structure shines when the workload is dominated by insertions and removals at the ends or at already-known positions?
ideal_answer: A linked list, which splices in O(1) at known positions, making it ideal for insertion/deletion-heavy workloads.

question: Which ordered tree has no balancing mechanism of its own, so its shape depends entirely on the order keys were inserted?
ideal_answer: A plain binary search tree, which does not self-balance, so its shape and performance depend on insertion order.

question: Feeding keys in already-sorted order into which tree yields a degenerate, list-like structure?
ideal_answer: A plain binary search tree, which becomes skewed (list-like, O(n)) when keys arrive sorted.

question: Which structure is best understood as the unbalanced baseline that self-balancing trees improve upon?
ideal_answer: A plain binary search tree, the unbalanced baseline that AVL and other self-balancing trees improve on.

question: In which tree may the heights of any node's left and right subtrees differ by at most one?
ideal_answer: An AVL tree, whose height-balance condition keeps each node's subtree heights within one of each other.

question: Which tree does extra rotation work on every update, making it slightly slower on write-heavy workloads?
ideal_answer: An AVL tree, which performs rotations on inserts/deletes to stay strictly balanced, costing extra work on writes.

question: Which structure guarantees logarithmic operations even when the data arrives in sorted or adversarial order?
ideal_answer: An AVL tree, whose self-balancing rotations guarantee worst-case O(log n) regardless of input order.

question: In which array-backed tree do the children of index i sit at positions 2i+1 and 2i+2?
ideal_answer: A binary heap, a complete tree stored in an array where index i's children are at 2i+1 and 2i+2.

question: Which structure is the engine behind heapsort and the usual implementation of a priority queue?
ideal_answer: A binary heap, which underlies heapsort and is the standard priority-queue implementation.

question: Which tree orders each parent only against its own children, so an in-order walk of it is meaningless?
ideal_answer: A binary heap, which only enforces the parent-child heap property and has no meaningful in-order ordering.

question: Which complete tree lets you read the current minimum in O(1) but pays O(log n) to insert or extract it?
ideal_answer: A binary heap: the root min/max is O(1) to read, while insert and extract are O(log n) as elements sift.

question: Which sort defuses its quadratic worst case by randomizing the pivot or using the median-of-three rule?
ideal_answer: Quicksort, whose worst case is made unlikely by pivot randomization or median-of-three selection.

question: Which divide-and-conquer sort partitions the data around a chosen element rather than merging sorted halves?
ideal_answer: Quicksort, which sorts by partitioning around a pivot instead of merging sorted halves.

question: Which sort is preferable when memory is tight and average-case speed matters more than worst-case guarantees?
ideal_answer: Quicksort, the in-place choice when memory is constrained and average speed beats worst-case guarantees.

question: Which in-place sort needs only about O(log n) stack space and no large auxiliary array?
ideal_answer: Quicksort, which sorts in place using only O(log n) recursion stack space and no big auxiliary array.

question: Which sort combines two already-sorted halves by repeatedly taking the smaller of the two front elements?
ideal_answer: Merge sort, whose merge step repeatedly takes the smaller front element of the two sorted halves.

question: Which comparison sort adapts well to data too large for memory and to linked lists, needing no random access?
ideal_answer: Merge sort, which works for external (out-of-memory) data and linked lists because merging needs no random access.

question: Which sort delivers a dependable worst-case bound and keeps equal keys in order, provided you can spare linear extra space?
ideal_answer: Merge sort: guaranteed O(n log n) and stable, at the cost of an O(n) auxiliary array.

question: Which divide-and-conquer sort is stable because, on ties, its merge step takes from the left half first?
ideal_answer: Merge sort, which preserves the order of equal elements by favoring the left half on ties during merging.

question: Which collision strategy tolerates a mediocre hash function and degrades gracefully as its lists grow?
ideal_answer: Separate chaining, which stays workable under a poor hash function and high load, degrading gradually as chains lengthen.

question: Which approach spends extra memory on list nodes and pointers and has worse cache locality than storing entries directly in the table?
ideal_answer: Separate chaining, whose per-bucket lists add node/pointer overhead and hurt cache locality versus in-table storage.

question: When deletions are frequent, which collision scheme handles them simply by removing an entry from a bucket's list?
ideal_answer: Separate chaining, where deletion just removes the entry from its bucket's list — no tombstones needed.

question: Which collision strategy finds a free slot using linear probing, quadratic probing, or double hashing?
ideal_answer: Open addressing, which resolves collisions by probing other slots via linear, quadratic, or double hashing.

question: Which approach keeps every entry inside the array itself, giving excellent cache locality and no pointer overhead?
ideal_answer: Open addressing, which stores entries directly in the array for great cache locality and zero pointer overhead.

question: Why must this collision scheme keep its load factor well below one for its probe sequences to terminate?
ideal_answer: Open addressing needs empty slots so probing can stop, so its load factor must stay well below one and it resizes as it fills.

question: Which collection only lets you touch the most recently added item, removing things in the reverse of the order they went in?
ideal_answer: A stack, a last-in-first-out collection where only the top element is accessible.

question: Which structure backs the function call stack and an editor's undo history?
ideal_answer: A stack, whose LIFO order models call frames and undo.

question: Which O(1) collection supports just push and pop at a single end?
ideal_answer: A stack, which pushes and pops only at its top.

question: Which structure holds the frontier of an iterative depth-first search?
ideal_answer: A stack, used as the LIFO frontier of an iterative DFS.

question: Which collection always serves whichever item has been waiting the longest?
ideal_answer: A queue, a first-in-first-out collection that serves the oldest waiting item next.

question: Which structure inserts at the rear and removes from the front, often implemented as a circular buffer?
ideal_answer: A queue, which enqueues at the rear and dequeues at the front, commonly as a circular buffer.

question: Which structure buffers work between a fast producer and a slow consumer while preserving arrival order?
ideal_answer: A queue, whose FIFO order preserves arrival order when buffering producer/consumer work.

question: Which structure holds the frontier of a breadth-first search?
ideal_answer: A queue, used as the FIFO frontier of a BFS.

question: Which self-balancing search tree enforces balance with node colors rather than strict subtree heights?
ideal_answer: A red-black tree, which uses red/black coloring rules instead of an explicit height-balance condition.

question: Which ordered tree performs fewer rotations per update than AVL, trading slightly slower lookups for faster inserts and deletes?
ideal_answer: A red-black tree, which balances more loosely than AVL, so it rotates less on updates.

question: Which balanced search tree backs standard-library ordered maps such as C++ std::map and Java TreeMap?
ideal_answer: A red-black tree, the usual implementation behind library ordered maps and sets.

question: In which tree must every root-to-leaf path contain the same number of black nodes, with no red node having a red child?
ideal_answer: A red-black tree, whose invariants include equal black-height per path and no red-red parent-child.

question: Which sorting algorithm repeatedly removes the maximum from a heap to place elements in order, sorting in place with a guaranteed worst case?
ideal_answer: Heapsort, which builds a heap and repeatedly extracts the max, in place and O(n log n) worst case.

question: Which O(n log n) sort needs only O(1) extra space yet is not stable and has poor cache locality?
ideal_answer: Heapsort, which is in-place and worst-case O(n log n) but unstable with scattered memory access.

question: Which sort gives quicksort's in-place memory profile together with merge sort's worst-case guarantee?
ideal_answer: Heapsort, combining in-place sorting with a guaranteed O(n log n) worst case.

question: Which sort is often slower in practice than quicksort despite the same asymptotic bound, because of its non-sequential access pattern?
ideal_answer: Heapsort, whose scattered access gives worse cache locality than quicksort.

question: Which simple sort runs in nearly linear time on almost-sorted data and is used for small subarrays inside bigger sorts?
ideal_answer: Insertion sort, which is adaptive (near O(n) on nearly-sorted input) and used as a small-case base.

question: Which O(n squared) sort builds a sorted prefix by sliding each new element back into place, and is both stable and in place?
ideal_answer: Insertion sort, which inserts each element into the sorted prefix; stable and in-place.

question: Which sort do optimized libraries switch to for very small arrays because of its low constant factors?
ideal_answer: Insertion sort, preferred on tiny arrays for its small constants.

question: Which quadratic sort is adaptive, stable, and needs only constant extra space?
ideal_answer: Insertion sort, which is adaptive on nearly-sorted data, stable, and O(1) space.

question: Which graph traversal finds the fewest-edge path in an unweighted graph by exploring level by level?
ideal_answer: Breadth-first search, which expands by distance and so finds shortest unweighted paths.

question: Which traversal uses a FIFO queue for its frontier and visits vertices in order of increasing distance from the source?
ideal_answer: Breadth-first search, which uses a queue and visits in distance order.

question: Which graph search can use a lot of memory because its frontier may hold an entire level of vertices?
ideal_answer: Breadth-first search, whose level-wide frontier can be a large fraction of the vertices.

question: Which traversal is best when the goal is likely close to the source and you want the smallest hop count?
ideal_answer: Breadth-first search, ideal for nearby goals and fewest-edge paths.

question: Which graph traversal plunges deep along one branch and backtracks, using recursion or a stack?
ideal_answer: Depth-first search, which goes deep then backtracks, using a stack or recursion.

question: Which traversal produces a topological ordering and detects cycles in a directed graph?
ideal_answer: Depth-first search, used for topological sort and cycle detection.

question: Which graph search uses memory proportional to the longest path rather than the width of a level?
ideal_answer: Depth-first search, whose memory is the recursion/stack depth (longest path).

question: Which traversal finds strongly connected components and risks stack overflow on very deep graphs?
ideal_answer: Depth-first search, used for strongly connected components and bounded by stack depth.

question: Which structure stores strings by their characters so all keys sharing a prefix share the same initial path?
ideal_answer: A trie (prefix tree), which branches on characters so shared prefixes share a path.

question: Which structure efficiently answers autocomplete and 'all words with this prefix' queries that a hash table cannot?
ideal_answer: A trie, which supports prefix listing and autocomplete that hashing cannot do efficiently.

question: In which structure does a lookup take time proportional to the key's length, with no hashing and no collisions?
ideal_answer: A trie, where operations cost O(key length) and there is no hashing or collision.

question: Which string structure can waste a lot of memory because each node may hold one child pointer per possible character?
ideal_answer: A trie, whose per-character child pointers make a sparse trie memory-hungry.

question: Which probabilistic structure can give false positives but never false negatives when testing set membership?
ideal_answer: A Bloom filter, which has one-sided error: possible false positives, never false negatives.

question: Which structure tests membership using a bit array and several hash functions, storing no actual keys?
ideal_answer: A Bloom filter, a bit array plus multiple hash functions that stores only bits.

question: Which space-efficient structure is used as a cheap pre-check to avoid expensive lookups for absent keys?
ideal_answer: A Bloom filter, used as a front-line membership check before a costly lookup.

question: Which membership structure cannot, in its standard form, delete elements or enumerate what it contains?
ideal_answer: A Bloom filter, which in its standard form supports neither deletion nor enumeration.
